system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

{"output": "=== paragu-ai-builder workflow contents ===
--- ci.yml ---
name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  pull_request:

jobs:
  quality:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: web

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: 'npm'
          cache-dependency-path: web/package-lock.json

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci --legacy-peer-deps

      - name: TypeScript check
        run: npx tsc --noEmit

      - name: Lint
        run: npx next lint --max-warnings 50

      - name: Check generated renderer map is up to date
        run: npm run generate:renderer && git diff --exit-code web/lib/engine/generated/renderer-map.ts

      - name: Build
        run: npm run build
        env:
          NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL: ${{ vars.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL }}
          NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY: ${{ vars.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY }}
          NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL: ${{ vars.NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL || 'https://paragu-ai.com' }}
===
--- deploy.yml ---
name: Deploy

# Cloudflare Pages auto-deploys from the GitHub repo — no CI token needed.
# This workflow only handles the VPS (Docker Swarm) deployment.
on:
  push:
    branches: [Main]
  workflow_dispatch:

concurrency:
  group: deploy-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  vps:
    name: VPS Hostinger
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 30
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - name: Setup SSH
        shell: bash
        run: |
          set +e
          mkdir -p ~/.ssh
          echo "${{ secrets.VPS_SSH_KEY }}" > ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
          chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
          ssh-keyscan -H 72.61.44.159 >> ~/.ssh/known_hosts 2>/dev/null || true

      - name: Run deploy script on VPS
        run: |
          ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new root@72.61.44.159 'bash /opt/stacks/paragu-ai-builder/deploy.sh prod'
===
--- pr-checks.yml ---
name: PR Checks

# Single merge gate for all PRs. Replaces the old set of overlapping
# workflows (ci.yml, ci-cd.yml, test.yml, validate.yml, quality-gates.yml)
# which all ran lint + typecheck + tests separately and wasted compute.
#
# Required on Main and staging via branch protection rules.

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [Main, staging]
  push:
    branches: [Main, staging]

concurrency:
  # Cancel superseded runs on the same PR — saves compute when multiple
  # pushes land quickly. Main/staging pushes don't cancel each other.
  group: pr-checks-${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' }}

jobs:
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # 1. Lint + Typecheck — cheapest, runs first, blocks other jobs
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  lint-typecheck:
    name: Lint & Typecheck
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 10
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: web
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: npm
          cache-dependency-path: web/package-lock.json
      - run: npm ci
      - name: Lint
        run: npm run lint
      - name: Typecheck
        run: npm run typecheck

   # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   #2. Schema validation — ensures every registry entry has a schema
   # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   validate-schemas:
     name: Validate Schemas
     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
     timeout-minutes: 5
     needs: lint-typecheck
     defaults:
       run:
         working-directory: ./
     steps:
       - uses: actions/checkout@v4
       - name: Validate registry has schemas
         run: npm run validate:schemas

   # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   #3. Tenant-config validation — catches bad page JSON / content refs /
   #    missing locale content before a runtime error bites a tenant.
   # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   validate:
     name: Validate Tenant Configs
     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
     timeout-minutes: 10
     needs: [lint-typecheck, validate-schemas]
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: web
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: npm
          cache-dependency-path: web/package-lock.json
      - run: npm ci
      - name: Validate tenant sites
        # TEMPORARY: allow machine-translated locales until nexa-paraguay's
        # German is professionally translated (tracked in sites/nexa-paraguay/
        # docs/LAUNCH.md item #8).
        env:
          ALLOW_MACHINE_TRANSLATIONS: '1'
        run: npm run validate:sites
      - name: Validate content placeholders
        run: npm run validate:content
      - name: Validate relocacion vertical
        run: npm run validate:vertical relocacion

  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # 3. Unit + integration tests with coverage upload
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  test:
    name: Unit Tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 15
    needs: lint-typecheck
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: web
    env:
      # Placeholders so code that reads env at module-load doesn't crash.
      # No real Supabase calls happen in unit tests.
      NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL: https://placeholder.supabase.co
      NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY: placeholder-anon-key
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: npm
          cache-dependency-path: web/package-lock.json
      - run: npm ci
      - name: Run tests
        run: npm run test:unit
      - name: Upload coverage
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: coverage
          path: web/coverage/
          retention-days: 7

  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # 4. Build smoke — verifies `npm run build` succeeds end-to-end.
  #    Runs after lint+typecheck to avoid wasting a slow build when
  #    the cheap checks already failed.
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  build:
    name: Build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 15
    needs: lint-typecheck
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: web
    env:
      NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL: https://placeholder.supabase.co
      NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY: placeholder-anon-key
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: npm
          cache-dependency-path: web/package-lock.json
      - run: npm ci
      - name: Build
        run: npm run build

  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # 5. Secrets scan — blocks PRs that accidentally commit API keys /
  #    tokens. TruffleHog flags high-entropy strings above threshold.
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  secrets-scan:
    name: Secrets Scan
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 10
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          # Full history so TruffleHog can diff base..HEAD.
          fetch-depth: 0
      - name: TruffleHog Secrets Scan
        uses: trufflesecurity/trufflehog@main
        with:
          base: ${{ github.event.repository.default_branch }}
          head: HEAD
          # `--only-verified` reports only secrets TruffleHog could
          # confirm against their originating service. Quieter than the
          # entropy heuristic and doesn't have the CLI-arg bug that was
          # failing every PR (--filter-entropy requires a value).
          extra_args: --only-verified

--- nightly.yml ---
name: Nightly

# Off-path checks that shouldn't block PRs but are worth knowing about.
# Runs once a day at ~03:00 UTC (00:00 Asunción). Also on manual dispatch.

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 3 * * *'
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # Dependency audit — flags known CVEs in our deps. Doesn't fail the
  # build (audit has a lot of noise at `moderate`), just logs + creates
  # an issue when `high` or `critical` show up so a human can triage.
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  audit:
    name: npm audit
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 10
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: web
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: npm
          cache-dependency-path: web/package-lock.json
      - run: npm ci
      - name: Audit (high+)
        # Fails only on high/critical — moderate CVEs are logged and
        # checked manually in the review.
        run: npm audit --audit-level=high

  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # Full E2E (moved off the PR path — Playwright takes ~8 min and has
  # historical flakiness so it lived as `continue-on-error` before).
  # Runs nightly against the built app. If we start trusting it, promote
  # back to pr-checks.yml.
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  e2e:
    name: Playwright E2E
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 30
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: web
    env:
      NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL: https://placeholder.supabase.co
      NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY: placeholder-anon-key
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: npm
          cache-dependency-path: web/package-lock.json
      - run: npm ci
      - name: Install Playwright browsers
        run: npx playwright install --with-deps chromium
      - name: Run E2E
        run: npm run test:e2e
        continue-on-error: true
      - name: Upload report
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: e2e-report
          path: web/playwright-report
          retention-days: 14

name: Lighthouse CI

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'web/components/**'
      - 'web/lib/engine/**'
      - 'web/lib/tokens/**'
      - 'web/app/**'
      - 'sites/**'
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  lighthouse:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: web
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'
          cache-dependency-path: web/package-lock.json

      - name: Install
        run: npm ci

      - name: Install Lighthouse CI
        run: npm i -g @lhci/cli@0.14.x

      - name: Build + run Lighthouse against 3 representative pages
        env:
          NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL: http://localhost:3000
        run: lhci autorun --config=./lighthouserc.json

      - name: Upload Lighthouse report
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: lighthouse-report
          path: web/.lighthouseci/
          retention-days: 7

--- cache-purge.yml ---
name: Cache purge (post-merge)

# Purges Cloudflare edge cache after a Main merge lands. Without this,
# the public site serves stale content for up to 24 hours via our
# stale-while-revalidate config (s-maxage=60, SWR=86400). Relies on:
#
#   Secrets:
#     CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN  — scoped to "Zone:Cache Purge"
#     CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID    — paragu-ai.com zone id
#
# If either secret is missing the job is a no-op (exit 0) so this
# workflow never blocks a merge.

on:
  push:
    branches: [Main]
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  purge:
    name: Purge Cloudflare cache
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 3
    steps:
      - name: Purge everything
        env:
          CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN }}
          CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID: ${{ secrets.CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID }}
        run: |
          if [ -z "$CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN" ] || [ -z "$CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID" ]; then
            echo "::notice::Cache-purge secrets not configured — skipping purge."
            exit 0
          fi

          response=$(curl -sS -X POST \
            "https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/zones/$CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID/purge_cache" \
            -H "Authorization: Bearer $CLOUD...KEN" \
            -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
            --data '{"purge_everything":true}')

          echo "$response"
          success=$(echo "$response" | grep -o '"success":[^,}]*' | head -1 | cut -d: -f2 | tr -d ' ')
          if [ "$success" != "true" ]; then
            echo "::error::Cache purge failed"
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "::notice::Cache purged successfully"

--- preview-deploy.yml ---
name: Preview Deploy

# Deploys every PR branch to a unique Cloudflare Pages preview URL
# so clients and team can review changes before merging.

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened]

concurrency:
  group: preview-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  preview:
    name: Deploy preview
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 15
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: web
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: npm
          cache-dependency-path: web/package-lock.json

      - name: Install
        run: npm ci --legacy-peer-deps

      - name: TypeScript check
        run: npx tsc --noEmit

      - name: Build
        run: npm run build
        env:
          NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL: ${{ vars.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL }}
          NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY: ${{ vars.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY }}
          NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL: ${{ vars.NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL || 'https://paragu-ai.com' }}

      - name: Deploy to Cloudflare Pages preview
        uses: cloudflare/wrangler-action@v3
        with:
          apiToken: ${{ secrets.CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN }}
          accountId: ${{ vars.CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID }}
          command: pages deploy .open-next --project-name=paragu-ai-builder --branch=${{ github.head_ref || github.ref_name }}

      - name: Comment PR with preview URL
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const pr = context.payload.pull_request;
            if (!pr) return;
            const branch = pr.head.ref;
            const previewUrl = `https://${branch}.paragu-ai-builder.pages.dev`;
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: pr.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: `🚀 Preview deployed: [${previewUrl}](${previewUrl})`,
            });

name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [master]
  pull_request:
    branches: [master]

permissions:
  contents: read

concurrency:
  group: ci-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Check YAML syntax
        run: |
          for f in stacks/**/docker-stack.yml stacks/**/docker-compose.yml configs/**/*.yml; do
            python3 -c "import yaml, sys; yaml.safe_load(open('$f'))" 2>/dev/null || echo "INVALID: $f"
          done

      - name: Check shell scripts
        run: |
          for f in scripts/*.sh deploy.sh; do
            [ -f "$f" ] && bash -n "$f" || echo "SYNTAX ERROR: $f"
          done

      - name: Check Python syntax
        run: |
          for f in scripts/*.py services/**/app.py; do
            [ -f "$f" ] && python3 -m py_compile "$f" 2>/dev/null || echo "SYNTAX ERROR: $f"
          done

      - name: Detect secrets
        uses: trufflesecurity/trufflehog@main
        with:
          path: ./
          base: ${{ github.event.repository.default_branch }}
          head: HEAD

  docker-compose-validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install Docker Compose
        run: |
          mkdir -p ~/.docker/cli-plugins
          curl -SL https://github.com/docker/compose/releases/download/v2.24.0/docker-compose-linux-x86_64 -o ~/.docker/cli-plugins/docker-compose
          chmod +x ~/.docker/cli-plugins/docker-compose

      - name: Validate Docker configs
        run: |
          for f in stacks/**/docker-stack.yml stacks/**/docker-compose.yml; do
            docker compose -f "$f" config --quiet && echo "OK: $f" || echo "INVALID: $f"
          done

  gitignore-check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Check .env is gitignored
        run: |
          grep -q '^\.env$' .gitignore && echo ".env is gitignored" || (echo ".env NOT gitignored!"; exit 1)

# ParaguAI — Operations Base

**Single source of truth.** Repos, clients, sites, infra, cron, contacts, issues.

## Index

| File | What's Inside |
|------|---------------|
| [client-repos.md](client-repos.md) | Every repo: client, framework, last commit, GH link, status, deep details |
| [clients.md](clients.md) | All clients with URLs, contacts, tiers |
| [cron.md](cron.md) | All 22 cron jobs with schedules and status |
| [infrastructure.md](infrastructure.md) | Docker Swarm, Traefik, network map |
| [issues.md](issues.md) | Priority-ordered 🔴🟡🟢 issues |
| [repos.md](repos.md) | Full repo index by category |

## Quick Stats

- **19 live websites** on *.paragu-ai.com + 2 on *.sunstein.cloud
- **22 Docker Swarm services** running on 1 VPS
- **30+ Git repos** (16 client sites, 9 platform, 5 Hermes, rest research/auto)
- **22 cron jobs** — all currently in ERROR state
- **37 GitHub repos** in Ai-Whisperers org (some archived)

Here's the definitive analysis and decision framework.

---

## Complete Diagnosis

### The 5 Real Problems

**1. You have TWO platforms fighting each other**
- `paragu-ai-builder` (8.4M TS, 478MB) — AI multi-tenant builder, generates sites under `/sites/`, has full CI/CD
- `paragu-ai` monorepo — supposed to be the shared platform, but has NO npm workspaces, is effectively dead code
- Neither talks to the other. Builder regenerates sites, monorepo just sits there.

**2. 17/19 client sites have zero CI/CD**
- Only elviajero-comercio and fun4me deploy via GH Actions
- All others: manual or cron. Deploying a client site is a copy-paste operation

**3. Massive code duplication without a release mechanism**
- elviajero has 104 components. golden-visa has 3. superspuma has 8.
- `client-kit` exists (5 components) but isn't published to npm — every repo has to copy it locally
- Updating a shared component means touching every repo manually

**4. paragu-ai monorepo is a hollow shell**
- Has `apps/clinica-duerksen` as a subfolder but no workspaces config
- No turbo.json, no shared build, no packages directory
- It's a folder structure pretending to be a monorepo

**5. No security layer at all**
- 0 org secrets, 0 branch protections, 0 environments, 0 webhooks
- SSH keys embedded in workflow YAML
- Any compromised token = full infrastructure access

### The Leverage Point

Your builder works. The constraint isn't tech — it's that every site is disconnected. The fix isn't to rebuild anything. It's to **extract the shared code into a publishable package** and **add CI/CD to every client site**.

---

## Two Paths

### Path A: The Org Overhaul (already planned)
The plan at `/root/aiw-org-overhaul-plan.md` — create `@aiw/base` monorepo with 8 npm packages (ui, commerce, admin, auth, whatsapp, seo, i18n, theme), extract from elviajero, install in all clients.

- **Cost:** 10-12 days, 8 packages, archive 18 repos
- **Benefit:** Centralized upgrades, consistent UX, reduced code
- **Risk:** Big upfront investment, builder-generated sites (under `/sites/`) won't benefit until rebuilt

### Path B: Ship the Builder Outputs + Minimal Wrapper
Leave the builder as-is. Make each generated site independently deployable with a thin wrapper:
1. Each site gets a `Dockerfile` + `deploy.yml` (copy from fun4me's pattern ~20 lines)
2. Add a shared `@ai-whisperers/client-kit` published to npm (5-10 components, not 8 packages)
3. CI/CD to every client repo in 1 day

- **Cost:** 1-2 days to set up CI/CD across all clients
- **Benefit:** Every site deployable, shared components published, minimal disruption
- **Risk:** Won't solve the builder vs monorepo split, slow for non-builder clients (elviajero)

---

**Which path do you want?** A) Full org overhaul (10-12 days, 8 packages, archive 18 repos) or B) Ship the builder + CI/CD to every client (1-2 days, one package, minimal change)

Or a hybrid: do B this week, then A when the builder clients stabilize.

{"output": "=== fun4me deploy.yml full ===
name: Deploy
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4
        with:
          version: 9
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 22
          cache: pnpm
      - run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
      - run: pnpm build
        env:
          NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL }}
          NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY: ${{ secrets.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY }}
          NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL: https://fun4me.paragu-ai.com
      - name: Deploy via SSH
        uses: appleboy/ssh-action@v1
        with:
          host: ${{ secrets.VPS_HOST }}
          username: ${{ secrets.VPS_USER }}
          key: ${{ secrets.VPS_KEY }}
          script: |
            cd /tmp && rm -rf fun4me && git clone https://github.com/Ai-Whisperers/fun4me.git

name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [master]
  pull_request:
    branches: [master]

permissions:
  contents: read

concurrency:
  group: ci-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Check YAML syntax
        run: |
          for f in stacks/**/docker-stack.yml stacks/**/docker-compose.yml configs/**/*.yml; do
            python3 -c "import yaml, sys; yaml.safe_load(open('$f'))" 2>/dev/null || echo "INVALID: $f"
          done

      - name: Check shell scripts
        run: |
          for f in scripts/*.sh deploy.sh; do
            [ -f "$f" ] && bash -n "$f" || echo "SYNTAX ERROR: $f"
          done

      - name: Check Python syntax
        run: |
          for f in scripts/*.py services/**/app.py; do
            [ -f "$f" ] && python3 -m py_compile "$f" 2>/dev/null || echo "SYNTAX ERROR: $f"
          done

      - name: Detect secrets
        uses: trufflesecurity/trufflehog@main
        with:
          path: ./
          base: ${{ github.event.repository.default_branch }}
          head: HEAD

  docker-compose-validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install Docker Compose
        run: |
          mkdir -p ~/.docker/cli-plugins
          curl -SL https://github.com/docker/compose/releases/download/v2.24.0/docker-compose-linux-x86_64 -o ~/.docker/cli-plugins/docker-compose
          chmod +x ~/.docker/cli-plugins/docker-compose

      - name: Validate Docker configs
        run: |
          for f in stacks/**/docker-stack.yml stacks/**/docker-compose.yml; do
            docker compose -f "$f" config --quiet && echo "OK: $f" || echo "INVALID: $f"
          done

  gitignore-check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Check .env is gitignored
        run: |
          grep -q '^\.env$' .gitignore && echo ".env is gitignored" || (echo ".env NOT gitignored!"; exit 1)
          grep -q '^\.env.template$' .gitignore && echo ".env.template is gitignored" || echo "NOTE: .env.template is intentionally not gitignored"", "exit_code": 0, "error": null}

{"path": "/root/aiw-github-update-plan.md", "content": "# AI Whisperers — Complete GitHub Update Plan

## Context

paragu-ai monorepo removed (was dead — no workspaces, not used).
67 repos total. 19 active client sites. Most have zero CI/CD.
Now: build a **reusable CI/CD repo** imported by all clients, harden security, clean up the org.

---

## Phase 1: Central CI/CD Repo (`ci-cd`)

**New repo:** `Ai-Whisperers/ci-cd`
**Purpose:** Single source of truth for all workflows. Update once, propagate everywhere.

### Structure

```
ci-cd/
├── .github/workflows/
│   ├── deploy-client-site.yml      ← reusable workflow (called by clients)
│   ├── deploy-paragu-ai-builder.yml
│   ├── ci-nextjs.yml               ← reusable: lint + typecheck + build + test
│   ├── ci-python.yml               ← reusable: ruff + pytest
│   ├── security-scan.yml           ← reusable: trufflehog + npm audit
│   └── sync-workflows.yml          ← pushes latest workflows to all client repos
├── scripts/
│   ├── deploy-vps.sh               ← the actual SSH deploy script (kept here, not in YAML)
│   └── sync-workflows.sh           ← copies .github/workflows/* to all client repos
├── templates/
│   ├── Dockerfile.nextjs           ← standard Next.js multi-stage Dockerfile
│   ├── docker-compose.yml          ← standard client Docker Compose
│   ├── .env.template
│   └── README.md
└── clients.json                    ← map of repo -> deploy config
```

### How It Works

1. **Reusable workflows** — each client repo adds ONE file `.github/workflows/deploy.yml`:
   ```yaml
   name: Deploy
   on:
     push:
       branches: [main]
   jobs:
     deploy:
       uses: Ai-Whisperers/ci-cd/.github/workflows/deploy-client-site.yml@main
       with:
         site-name: fun4me
         site-url: https://fun4me.paragu-ai.com
       secrets: inherit
   ```

2. **`sync-workflows` workflow** — pushes latest workflow versions to all client repos on push to ci-cd main. No manual copy-paste.

3. **`clients.json`** — central registry:
   ```json
   {
     "fun4me": {
       "repo": "Ai-Whisperers/fun4me",
       "url": "https://fun4me.paragu-ai.com",
       "package-manager": "pnpm",
       "node-version": "22",
       "docker-image": "fun4me:prod",
       "docker-service": "fun4me_web",
       "build-args": ["NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL", "NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY", "NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL"]
     },
     "elviajero-comercio": {
       "repo": "Ai-Whisperers/elviajero-comercio",
       "url": "https://viajero.paragu-ai.com",
       "package-manager": "npm",
       "node-version": "20",
       "docker-image": "elviajero-comercio:prod",
       "docker-service": "elviajero-comercio_web",
       "deploy-strategy": "git-pull"  // different from build-new below
     },
     ...
   }
   ```

### Workflow Matrix

| Workflow | Trigger | What It Does |
|----------|---------|--------------|
| `ci-nextjs.yml` | PR + push to main | npm/pnpm install → lint → typecheck → build → test → trufflehog |
| `ci-python.yml` | PR + push to main | ruff check → pytest → trufflehog |
| `deploy-client-site.yml` | push to main (called) | build Docker image on VPS → `docker service update --force` |
| `deploy-paragu-ai-builder.yml` | push to main | CF Pages deploy + VPS deploy |
| `security-scan.yml` | PR + scheduled weekly | trufflehog + npm audit + docker scan |
| `sync-workflows.yml` | push to ci-cd main | copies workflows to all client repos via GH API |

---

## Phase 2: Security Hardening

### Must Fix (week 1)
- [ ] **Set org-level secrets** — VPS_HOST, VPS_USER, VPS_KEY, CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN, SUPABASE_URL, SUPABASE_ANON_KEY
- [ ] **Update ci-cd workflows** to use org secrets via `secrets: inherit` — no more inline secrets in YAML files
- [ ] **Remove inline SSH keys** from elviajero and fun4me deploy.yml
- [ ] **Add branch protection** to elviajero, fun4me, paragu-ai-builder, paragu-ai-leads, ma-research-pipeline
  - Require PR for main, require status checks, require signed commits

### Should Fix (week 2)
- [ ] **GitHub Environments** — create `production` env with VPS_HOST/VPS_KEY required only from that env
- [ ] **Change default workflow permissions** to `read` from `write`
- [ ] **Add CODEOWNERS** to every active repo
- [ ] **Set up Dependabot** on all active repos (not just builder + infra)
- [ ] **Add security.md vulnerability reporting**
- [ ] **Audit repo access** — verify only needed members per repo

### Nice to Have
- [ ] **Webhook to Hermes** on security alert / Dependabot alert
- [ ] **Scheduled security audit** (1x/week via cron)
- [ ] **Signed commits enforcement**

---

## Phase 3: Org Cleanup

### Archive List (no longer needed)
- `stroopwafel-huis` — business plan, not code. Move to docs.
- `nicolas-duarte-career` — merged into nicolas-duarte-site
- `nico-duarte`, `nico` — empty, superseded
- `pizzeria-ejemplo` — example, not needed
- `grocery` — abandoned experiment
- `contract-analyzer` — not actively used
- `Johns-Grimoire` — personal notes
- `wizard-academy` — abandoned
- `Marketing-Agent` — abandoned
- `Company-Repos-Understanding` — one-off research
- All archived repos currently in org: confirm they stay archived

### Topics to Add (every active repo needs)
- `client-site`, `platform`, `tool`, `research`, `infrastructure`, `template`
- Language tags: `typescript`, `python`, `java`
- Business tags: `ecommerce`, `healthcare`, `legal`, `beauty`, `music`, `agency`

### Repo Naming Cleanup
- `3md-website` → `3md-creative-agency` (descriptive)
- `BDSM-Paraguay-Toolkit` → `bdsm-paraguay-toolkit` (lowercase, no caps)
- `Ivan_Weiss_Portfolio` → `ivan-weiss-portfolio` (consistent kebab-case)
- Note: renaming repos breaks URLs. Only do if worth it.

---

## Phase 4: Client CI/CD Rollout

One deploy.yml per client. Each client gets:

| Client | Repo | Node | PM | Deploy Strategy | Existing CI? |
|--------|------|------|----|----------------|-------------|
| El Viajero | elviajero-comercio | 20 | npm | git-pull + build on VPS | YES (deploy.yml) |

| Clinica Duerksen | clinica-duerksen | 20 | npm | build on VPS | NO |

**Estimate:** 1 hour to create ci-cd repo with reusable workflows. 15 min per client = ~5 hours total.

---

## Phase 5: Additional Workstreams

### 5a: client-kit → Published npm Package
- Current: 5 components, local-only, copied into repos
- Target: `@ai-whisperers/client-kit` published to npm
- Minimal scope: WhatsAppFloat, SEO component, analytics, theme tokens, error boundary
- Add CI/CD to client-kit repo: `npm publish` on tag push
- Install in all 19 client repos in one batch command

### 5b: template-nextjs-client Refresh

### 5c: Priorities Repo Format
- Currently: markdown files. Working. Keep as-is — it's the SSOT.
- Add: `clients.json` in machine-readable format (parallel to markdown for tool consumption)

### 5d: Monitoring (aiw-infra)
- Prometheus + Grafana stack exists in `stacks/monitoring/` but not confirmed running

## Phase 6: Org-Level Automation

### Webhooks

### Release Strategy

## Timeline

Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:

# GitHub Repository Management

Create, clone, fork, configure, and manage GitHub repositories. Each section shows `gh` first, then the `git` + `curl` fallback.

## Prerequisites

- Authenticated with GitHub (see `github-auth` skill)

### Setup

```bash
if command -v gh &>/dev/null && gh auth status &>/dev/null; then
  AUTH="gh"
else
  AUTH="git"
  if [ -z "$GITHUB_TOKEN" ]; then
    if [ -f ~/.hermes/.env ] && grep -q "^GITHUB_TOKEN=" ~/.hermes/.env; then
      GITHUB_TOKEN=$(grep "^GITHUB_TOKEN=" ~/.hermes/.env | head -1 | cut -d= -f2 | tr -d '\n\r')
    elif grep -q "github.com" ~/.git-credentials 2>/dev/null; then
      GITHUB_TOKEN=$(grep "github.com" ~/.git-credentials 2>/dev/null | head -1 | sed 's|https://[^:]*:\([^@]*\)@.*|\1|')
    fi
  fi
fi

# Get your GitHub username (needed for several operations)
if [ "$AUTH" = "gh" ]; then
  GH_USER=$(gh api user --jq '.login')
else
  GH_USER=$(curl -s -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" https://api.github.com/user | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['login'])")
fi
```

If you're inside a repo already:

```bash
REMOTE_URL=$(git remote get-url origin)
OWNER_REPO=$(echo "$REMOTE_URL" | sed -E 's|.*github\.com[:/]||; s|\.git$||')
OWNER=$(echo "$OWNER_REPO" | cut -d/ -f1)
REPO=$(echo "$OWNER_REPO" | cut -d/ -f2)
```

---

## 1. Cloning Repositories

Cloning is pure `git` — works identically either way:

```bash
# Clone via HTTPS (works with credential helper or token-embedded URL)
git clone https://github.com/owner/repo-name.git

# Clone into a specific directory
git clone https://github.com/owner/repo-name.git ./my-local-dir

# Shallow clone (faster for large repos)
git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/owner/repo-name.git

# Clone a specific branch
git clone --branch develop https://github.com/owner/repo-name.git

# Clone via SSH (if SSH is configured)
git clone [REDACTED_EMAIL]:owner/repo-name.git
```

**With gh (shorthand):**

```bash
gh repo clone owner/repo-name
gh repo clone owner/repo-name -- --depth 1
```

## 2. Creating Repositories

**With gh:**

```bash
# Create a public repo and clone it
gh repo create my-new-project --public --clone

# Private, with description and license
gh repo create my-new-project --private --description "A useful tool" --license MIT --clone

# Under an organization
gh repo create my-org/my-new-project --public --clone

# From existing local directory
cd /path/to/existing/project
gh repo create my-project --source . --public --push
```

**With git + curl:**

```bash
# Create the remote repo via API
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/user/repos \
  -d '{
    "name": "my-new-project",
    "description": "A useful tool",
    "private": false,
    "auto_init": true,
    "license_template": "mit"
  }'

# Clone it
git clone https://github.com/$GH_USER/my-new-project.git
cd my-new-project

# -- OR -- push an existing local directory to the new repo
cd /path/to/existing/project
git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit"
git remote add origin https://github.com/$GH_USER/my-new-project.git
git push -u origin main
```

To create under an organization:

```bash
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/orgs/my-org/repos \
  -d '{"name": "my-new-project", "private": false}'
```

### From a Template

**With gh:**

```bash
gh repo create my-new-app --template owner/template-repo --public --clone
```

**With curl:**

```bash
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/owner/template-repo/generate \
  -d '{"owner": "'"$GH_USER"'", "name": "my-new-app", "private": false}'
```

## 3. Forking Repositories

**With gh:**

```bash
gh repo fork owner/repo-name --clone
```

**With git + curl:**

```bash
# Create the fork via API
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/owner/repo-name/forks

# Wait a moment for GitHub to create it, then clone
sleep 3
git clone https://github.com/$GH_USER/repo-name.git
cd repo-name

# Add the original repo as "upstream" remote
git remote add upstream https://github.com/owner/repo-name.git
```

### Keeping a Fork in Sync

```bash
# Pure git — works everywhere
git fetch upstream
git checkout main
git merge upstream/main
git push origin main
```

**With gh (shortcut):**

```bash
gh repo sync $GH_USER/repo-name
```

## 4. Repository Information

**With gh:**

```bash
gh repo view owner/repo-name
gh repo list --limit 20
gh search repos "machine learning" --language python --sort stars
```

**With curl:**

```bash
# View repo details
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
r = json.load(sys.stdin)
print(f\"Name: {r['full_name']}\")
print(f\"Description: {r['description']}\")
print(f\"Stars: {r['stargazers_count']}  Forks: {r['forks_count']}\")
print(f\"Default branch: {r['default_branch']}\")
print(f\"Language: {r['language']}\")"

# List your repos
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  "https://api.github.com/user/repos?per_page=20&sort=updated" \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
for r in json.load(sys.stdin):
    vis = 'private' if r['private'] else 'public'
    print(f\"  {r['full_name']:40}  {vis:8}  {r.get('language', ''):10}  ★{r['stargazers_count']}\")"

# Search repos
curl -s \
  "https://api.github.com/search/repositories?q=machine+learning+language:python&sort=stars&per_page=10" \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
for r in json.load(sys.stdin)['items']:
    print(f\"  {r['full_name']:40}  ★{r['stargazers_count']:6}  {r['description'][:60] if r['description'] else ''}\")"
```

## 5. Repository Settings

**With gh:**

```bash
gh repo edit --description "Updated description" --visibility public
gh repo edit --enable-wiki=false --enable-issues=true
gh repo edit --default-branch main
gh repo edit --add-topic "machine-learning,python"
gh repo edit --enable-auto-merge
```

**With curl:**

```bash
curl -s -X PATCH \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO \
  -d '{
    "description": "Updated description",
    "has_wiki": false,
    "has_issues": true,
    "allow_auto_merge": true
  }'

# Update topics
curl -s -X PUT \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  -H "Accept: application/vnd.github.mercy-preview+json" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/topics \
  -d '{"names": ["machine-learning", "python", "automation"]}'
```

## 6. Branch Protection

```bash
# View current protection
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/branches/main/protection

# Set up branch protection
curl -s -X PUT \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/branches/main/protection \
  -d '{
    "required_status_checks": {
      "strict": true,
      "contexts": ["ci/test", "ci/lint"]
    },
    "enforce_admins": false,
    "required_pull_request_reviews": {
      "required_approving_review_count": 1
    },
    "restrictions": null
  }'
```

## 7. Secrets Management (GitHub Actions)

**With gh:**

```bash
gh secret set API_KEY --body "your-secret-value"
gh secret set SSH_KEY < ~/.ssh/id_rsa
gh secret list
gh secret delete API_KEY
```

**With curl:**

Secrets require encryption with the repo's public key — more involved via API:

```bash
# Get the repo's public key for encrypting secrets
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/actions/secrets/public-key

# Encrypt and set (requires Python with PyNaCl)
python3 -c "
from base64 import b64encode
from nacl import encoding, public
import json, sys

# Get the public key
key_id = '<key_id_from_above>'
public_key = '<base64_key_from_above>'

# Encrypt
sealed = public.SealedBox(
    public.PublicKey(public_key.encode('utf-8'), encoding.Base64Encoder)
).encrypt('your-secret-value'.encode('utf-8'))
print(json.dumps({
    'encrypted_value': b64encode(sealed).decode('utf-8'),
    'key_id': key_id
}))"

# Then PUT the encrypted secret
curl -s -X PUT \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/actions/secrets/API_KEY \
  -d '<output from python script above>'

# List secrets (names only, values hidden)
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/actions/secrets \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
for s in json.load(sys.stdin)['secrets']:
    print(f\"  {s['name']:30}  updated: {s['updated_at']}\")"
```

Note: For secrets, `gh secret set` is dramatically simpler. If setting secrets is needed and `gh` isn't available, recommend installing it for just that operation.

## 8. Releases

**With gh:**

```bash
gh release create v1.0.0 --title "v1.0.0" --generate-notes
gh release create v2.0.0-rc1 --draft --prerelease --generate-notes
gh release create v1.0.0 ./dist/binary --title "v1.0.0" --notes "Release notes"
gh release list
gh release download v1.0.0 --dir ./downloads
```

**With curl:**

```bash
# Create a release
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/releases \
  -d '{
    "tag_name": "v1.0.0",
    "name": "v1.0.0",
    "body": "## Changelog\n- Feature A\n- Bug fix B",
    "draft": false,
    "prerelease": false,
    "generate_release_notes": true
  }'

# List releases
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/releases \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
for r in json.load(sys.stdin):
    tag = r.get('tag_name', 'no tag')
    print(f\"  {tag:15}  {r['name']:30}  {'draft' if r['draft'] else 'published'}\")"

# Upload a release asset (binary file)
RELEASE_ID=<id_from_create_response>
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/octet-stream" \
  "https://uploads.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/releases/$RELEASE_ID/assets?name=binary-amd64" \
  --data-binary @./dist/binary-amd64
```

## 9. GitHub Actions Workflows

**With gh:**

```bash
gh workflow list
gh run list --limit 10
gh run view <RUN_ID>
gh run view <RUN_ID> --log-failed
gh run rerun <RUN_ID>
gh run rerun <RUN_ID> --failed
gh workflow run ci.yml --ref main
gh workflow run deploy.yml -f environment=staging
```

**With curl:**

```bash
# List workflows
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/actions/workflows \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
for w in json.load(sys.stdin)['workflows']:
    print(f\"  {w['id']:10}  {w['name']:30}  {w['state']}\")"

# List recent runs
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  "https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/actions/runs?per_page=10" \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
for r in json.load(sys.stdin)['workflow_runs']:
    print(f\"  Run {r['id']}  {r['name']:30}  {r['conclusion'] or r['status']}\")"

# Download failed run logs
RUN_ID=<run_id>
curl -s -L \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/actions/runs/$RUN_ID/logs \
  -o /tmp/ci-logs.zip
cd /tmp && unzip -o ci-logs.zip -d ci-logs

# Re-run a failed workflow
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/actions/runs/$RUN_ID/rerun

# Re-run only failed jobs
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/actions/runs/$RUN_ID/rerun-failed-jobs

# Trigger a workflow manually (workflow_dispatch)
WORKFLOW_ID=<workflow_id_or_filename>
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/actions/workflows/$WORKFLOW_ID/dispatches \
  -d '{"ref": "main", "inputs": {"environment": "staging"}}'
```

## 10. Gists

**With gh:**

```bash
gh gist create script.py --public --desc "Useful script"
gh gist list
```

**With curl:**

```bash
# Create a gist
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/gists \
  -d '{
    "description": "Useful script",
    "public": true,
    "files": {
      "script.py": {"content": "print(\"hello\")"}
    }
  }'

# List your gists
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/gists \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
for g in json.load(sys.stdin):
    files = ', '.join(g['files'].keys())
    print(f\"  {g['id']}  {g['description'] or '(no desc)':40}  {files}\")"
```

## Quick Reference Table

| Action | gh | git + curl |
|--------|-----|-----------|
| Clone | `gh repo clone o/r` | `git clone https://github.com/o/r.git` |
| Create repo | `gh repo create name --public` | `curl POST /user/repos` |
| Fork | `gh repo fork o/r --clone` | `curl POST /repos/o/r/forks` + `git clone` |
| Repo info | `gh repo view o/r` | `curl GET /repos/o/r` |
| Edit settings | `gh repo edit --...` | `curl PATCH /repos/o/r` |
| Create release | `gh release create v1.0` | `curl POST /repos/o/r/releases` |
| List workflows | `gh workflow list` | `curl GET /repos/o/r/actions/workflows` |
| Rerun CI | `gh run rerun ID` | `curl POST /repos/o/r/actions/runs/ID/rerun` |
| Set secret | `gh secret set KEY` | `curl PUT /repos/o/r/actions/secrets/KEY` (+ encryption) |

# Org Architecture Planning

Design and execute a repo restructuring for a multi-repo GitHub organization.

## Workflow

### Step 1: Full Org Inventory

```bash
# Full repo list with all relevant fields (note: defaultBranch vs defaultBranchRef field name differs by gh version)
gh repo list ORG --limit 200 --json name,description,isFork,isPrivate,visibility,primaryLanguage,pushedAt,createdAt,isArchived,repositoryTopics
```

For each repo, also fetch languages:
```bash
gh api "repos/ORG/REPO/languages"
```

### Best Practice: Distinguish Personal Account from Org

Check both:
```bash
gh api /orgs/ORG-NAME  # 404 if it's a personal account, not an org
gh api /user --jq '{login,type,id}'  # 'User' vs 'Organization'
```

Personal accounts don't have org-level settings (teams, secrets, webhooks). Adjust expectations.

### Step 1b: Fetch All Org Config in One Pass

```bash
# Team info
gh api /orgs/ORG/teams --jq '.[] | {name,slug,privacy,description}'

# Members  
gh api /orgs/ORG/members --jq '.[].login'

# Org secrets (often 0)
gh api /orgs/ORG/actions/secrets --jq '.total_count as $c | .secrets[] | {name,visibility}'

# Actions permissions / security posture
gh api /orgs/ORG/actions/permissions --jq '{enabled_repositories,allowed_actions,sha_pinning_required}'
gh api /orgs/ORG/actions/permissions/workflow --jq '{default_workflow_permissions,can_approve_pull_request_reviews}'

# Webhooks
gh api /orgs/ORG/hooks --jq '.[] | {id,type,config:{url,content_type},events,active}'

# Runners
gh api /orgs/ORG/actions/runners --jq '.runners[] | {name,os,status,busy,labels:[.labels[].name]}'
```

### Step 1c: Enumerate All Environments + Branch Protection

```bash
# Environments across repos
for repo in repo1 repo2 repo3; do
  gh api "repos/ORG/$repo/environments" --jq '.environments[].name'
done

# Branch protection
gh api "repos/ORG/$repo/branches/main/protection" --jq '{required_status_checks,enforce_admins,required_pull_request_reviews,restrictions}'
```

### Step 2: Categorize Repos

Group into these categories:

| Category | Description | Examples |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| **core-platform** | Builder engines, monorepos | paragu-ai-builder, paragu-ai |
| **client-sites** | Production websites | elviajero, nexa, golden-visa |
| **templates** | Starter repos, shared components | template-nextjs-client, client-kit |
| **infra-tools** | DevOps, CI/CD, agents | priorities, code-agent, aiw-infra |
| **research** | Data pipelines, experiments | ma-research-pipeline, solstein |
| **docs** | Documentation repos | aiw-docs |
| **leads** | Auto-generated, experimental | magnolia-peluqueria, mantra-spa |
| **other** | Stale/archived/unrelated | energy21, telescope-ai |

### Step 3: Decide Action Per Repo

**ARCHIVE when:**
- Description says "[ARCHIVED]" or "Superseded by..."
- Zero disk usage (empty repos)
- Auto-generated with no updates (leads with 104KB identical size)
- Stale >30 days with no foreseeable work
- Content was migrated to another repo

**MERGE when:**
- Duplicate purpose (client-kit → template-nextjs-client)
- Same brand/product (nexa-propiedades → nexa-paraguay)
- Monorepo + standalone builder (paragu-ai → paragu-ai-builder)

**SPLIT when:**
- Monolith >100MB with multiple subsystems
- paragu-ai-builder pattern: engine + admin + builder packages

**FIX when:**
- Monorepo without workspaces (paragu-ai has only `typescript` as dep)
- Stale template (template-nextjs-client missing shadcn/ui, supabase, auth)

**KEEP when:**
- Active client site with regular updates
- Core infrastructure
- Research with ongoing work
- Public documentation

### Step 4: Design Monorepo Architecture

When the user wants "1 base repo for all client sites":

```
org-base/
├── packages/
│   ├── ui/              # shadcn/ui components (Button, Card, Dialog, DataTable, etc.)
│   ├── commerce/        # Cart, checkout, payment gateways, product cards
│   ├── admin/           # Admin layout, DataTable, stat cards, charts
│   ├── auth/            # Supabase auth (SSR, OAuth, admin role check)
│   ├── whatsapp/        # WhatsApp Float, templates, messaging
│   ├── seo/             # JSON-LD, sitemap, OG, analytics
│   ├── i18n/            # Translation helpers + locale management
│   └── config/          # Tailwind theme, design tokens, fonts
├── apps/
│   ├── template/        # Reference template (uses packages/*)
│   ├── elviajero/       # Client site (uses packages/*)
│   ├── golden-visa/     # Client site (uses packages/*)
│   ├── nexa/            # Client site (uses packages/*)
│   └── builder/         # Existing builder (moves here)
├── package.json         # npm workspaces
└── tsconfig.json        # Shared TS config
```

**Why monorepo (not separate packages):**
- Single `npm install` for all clients
- Types shared between packages
- Synchronized versions — one change reflects everywhere
- One CI/CD pipeline

### Step 5: Extract Shared Packages

Identify cross-repo duplicated code:

| Package | Source (most mature version) | Contains |
|---------|------------------------------|----------|
| `@org/ui` | builder UI + shadcn | Button, Card, Dialog, Toast, Skeleton, Badge, Tooltip, forms |
| `@org/commerce` | elviajero | CartSidebar, CheckoutStepper, ProductCard, PriceDisplay, AddToCart |
| `@org/admin` | elviajero | AdminLayout, StatCard, DataTable, ChartCard, ImageUpload |
| `@org/auth` | elviajero | AuthProvider, AuthGuard, login/register, OAuth |
| `@org/whatsapp` | builder + elviajero | WhatsAppFloat, share, message templates |
| `@org/seo` | builder | JsonLd, OG, sitemap, Analytics, CookieConsent |

El Viajero is always the best extraction source because it has the most mature implementations (86 components, 18 admin pages, full auth + payment + checkout).

### Step 6: Migration Order (Cold Turkey vs Gradual)

**Phase 1 — Create monorepo:**
```bash
mkdir org-base && cd org-base
npm init -w packages/ui -w packages/commerce -w packages/admin
git init
```

**Phase 2 — Extract from elviajero (first client):**
- Copy ui components to `packages/ui`
- Extract commerce components to `packages/commerce`
- Extract admin to `packages/admin`
- Extract auth to `packages/auth`
- Update elviajero imports from `@/components/` to `@org/ui`, `@org/commerce`, etc.

**Phase 3 — Migrate other sites gradually**
- Each site becomes an app, imports from packages
- Old repos archived after migration

### Step 7: Clean Up

After migration:
```bash
# Archive old repos
gh repo edit ORG/REPO --visibility private  # if public
gh api -X PATCH repos/ORG/REPO -f archived=true
```

## Pitfalls

- **Don't archive before migration.** Only archive after the new repo is live and deployed.
- **Check `diskUsage`** — repos with 0KB are truly empty and safe to archive immediately.
- **Auto-generated leads** all have identical size (~104KB) — they're clones. Never touch, just archive.
- **Public repos need private first** before archive (or leave public if docs/reference).
- **Monorepo with no workspaces** (paragu-ai) is broken by design — fix or delete, don't keep as-is.
- **Size check matters** — paragu-ai-builder at 479MB is the largest monolith. Splitting it is weeks of work, not hours.
- **Client sites don't all need to migrate** — 10-20 small static sites (<1MB each) are fine standalone. Only the complex ones (ecommerce, admin, auth) benefit from shared packages.
- **Archive in batches, not one-by-one.** Group all stale repos into a single commit/PR.
- **No branch protection is the norm** for early-stage orgs. Document it as a gap, don't fix it without user request.
- **Workflow files are the best signal for CI maturity.** Count workflows per repo. A repo with 8 workflows (like paragu-ai-builder) is the reference standard. Repos with 0 workflows are un-automated.
- **RepositoryTopics are often null** even when the API field exists. No need to set them unless the user asks.
- **Personal repos that became org repos** (like IvanWeissVanDerPol → Ai-Whisperers) may have stale personal copies. Cross-reference both accounts.
- **When analyzing, use `gh api` not `curl`** to avoid auth token management. The `gh` CLI handles token injection automatically.
- **Workflow YAML decoding**: `gh api repos/ORG/REPO/contents/.github/workflows/NAME --jq .content | base64 -d

## Package Extraction (Step 5 expanded)

When extracting packages from elviajero into a monorepo, follow this sequence:

### Import Rewriting Patterns

| Source Pattern | Package Pattern |
|---|---|
| `@/content/es.json` | Remove entirely; make component config/prop-driven |
| `@/lib/utils` | `./cn` (inline a cn utility) |
| `@/components/` | @org/ui or relative ./ |
| `@/lib/supabase/` | @org/auth/supabase/ |
| `@/lib/auth/` | @org/auth/ |

### Subpath Exports (required for deep imports)

Each package that exports submodules needs explicit `exports` in package.json:

```json
{
  "exports": {
    ".": { "import": "./dist/index.js", "types": "./dist/index.d.ts" },
    "./supabase/browser": { "import": "./dist/supabase/browser.js", "types": "./dist/supabase/browser.d.ts" },
    "./supabase/server": { "import": "./dist/supabase/server.js", "types": "./dist/supabase/server.d.ts" }
  }
}
```

TypeScript won't resolve `@org/auth/supabase/browser` without these entries.

### Extraction Order

1. Create monorepo with Turborepo + npm workspaces
2. Copy source files from elviajero to each package
3. Fix imports: remove @/content/es.json, fix relative paths
4. Set up tsconfig per package (extend base, add jsx: react-jsx)
5. Add subpath exports to package.json
6. Install deps per package (npm install -w packages/X)
7. Build deps first (auth before admin since admin imports auth)
8. Run turbo run build to verify all pass

### Common Errors

- **read_file line-number corruption**: The read_file tool returns content prefixed with LINE_NUM|. Never pipe output directly to write_file. Use terminal(cat path) for content you rewrite, or strip with re.sub(r'^\s*\d+\|', '', content, flags=re.MULTILINE).
- **@/content/es.json imports**: Build-time static imports. Replace with prop interfaces or remove entirely.
- **.ts extension in imports**: shadcn/ui files import ./cn.ts. TypeScript forbids this without allowImportingTsExtensions. Remove the .ts suffix.
- **Duplicate exports**: When auth-context and supabase/browser both export createClient, remove one re-export from index.ts.
- **next/headers cookies() API**: Next.js 15 made cookies() async. Must await before calling methods.
- **Missing sidebar reference**: Admin packages reference ./sidebar which may not exist in the source. Create a minimal sidebar or check if the component exists first.
- **Node modules in commits**: Always ensure .gitignore covers node_modules/, .turbo/, dist/, packages/*/node_modules/ before first commit.

The AdminShell provides: - Sidebar navigation with 4 groups: Operaciones, Catálogo, Marketing, Configuración - Mobile responsiveness (hidden sidebar on mobile, toggle with hamburger) - Auth guard via `useAdminAuth` - Consistent background and layout padding ## Auth Flow & Cookie Debugging ### The Core Trap: Two Auth Paths Crossing In Next.js + Supabase e-commerce projects, there are usually TWO auth paths that can conflict: **Path A — Consumer auth (useAuth):** - Login page calls `supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()` directly from the browser client - Supabase's `@supabase/ssr` browser client sets the session cookie automatically - `/mi-cuenta` reads session via `supabase.auth.getSession()` — works because cookie exists **Path B — Admin auth (useAdminAuth / /api/auth):** - Login page calls `/api/auth` with `action: "login"` - API route creates an SSR client with `createServerClient()`, but often has `setAll() {}` empty - **The session cookie never gets set on the response** — the API returns tokens, but no cookie - Admin layout falls back to localStorage or custom cookie **Common failure modes:** 1. **Login works (gets tokens) but /mi-cuenta shows unauthenticated** — consumer login page fetches `/api/auth` instead of calling Supabase directly. Fix: use `useAuth().login()` (Supabase browser client) which sets the correct session cookie. 2. **Login works but /admin redirects back to /login** — admin layout's `useAdminAuth()` calls `/api/auth?action=me` with a token from localStorage, but the API route's token verification path fails. Fix: ensure the API route can verify tokens against Supabase REST API (not just SSR cookies). ### Fix Checklist - [ ] Consumer login uses Supabase browser client directly (`supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()`), NOT a custom `/api/auth` endpoint - [ ] After successful login, store session tokens in `localStorage` for admin layout fallback: `localStorage.setItem("elviajero_admin_session", JSON.stringify({ access_token, refresh_token }))` - [ ] Admin layout checks localStorage first, then Supabase SSR cookie, then custom cookie — triple fallback - [ ] API route `action: "me"` verifies tokens via `fetch(${supabaseUrl}/auth/v1/user)` not `supabase.auth.getUser()` - [ ] Service-role client for profile queries uses `createClient(url, serviceRoleKey)` — passing service key as second arg, not in headers - [ ] Role check queries DB profiles table (not JWT claim) — `profiles.role` not `user.role` ### Debugging Auth Issues ```bash # Check if API route is reachable curl -sX POST https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com/api/auth \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"action":"login","email":"[REDACTED_EMAIL]","password":"test"}' | head -c 200 # Check if session cookie is set curl -sv https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com/mi-cuenta 2>&1 | grep -i 'set-cookie' # Check server logs docker service logs elviajero-comercio_web --tail 30 --no-task-ids ``` ## Visual Asset Workflow ### Full Asset Overhaul Pattern When doing a complete visual assets redesign (inventory, generation, deployment): #### Phase 1: Inventory ```bash # List all files on disk find public/images -type f | sort # List all code references grep -rohP '"/images/[^"'"'"']+' --include='*.{tsx,ts,json,css}' | sed 's/^"//' | sort -u # Cross-reference: 3 outcomes per image # 1. Then regenerate the worst offenders in the next round. #### Phase 4: Deployment Pitfalls **Cloudflare cache (IMPORTANT):** - CF sets `cache-control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable` — 1 YEAR cache - No CF API key? Only option is **rename the file** (logo.png → logo-v2.png) - Then update ALL code references to point to new filename - Verify with curl, not browser: `curl -sk URL/images/logo-v2.png -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} %{size_download}B"` - Then tell the user to hard refresh (Cmd+Shift+R) **Docker layer cache:** - If files exist locally but return 404 on deploy, the Docker COPY was cached - Fix: `docker build --no-cache` or touch a file in the COPY layer **Verify the right file is served:** ```bash # Check HTML references curl -sk https://example.com/ | grep -oP '(logo|favicon)-v2' # Check file directly curl -sk https://example.com/images/logo-v2.png -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} %{size_download}B" ``` ### Key Project-Specific Patterns (El Viajero) - Product images: `content/es.json` → `/images/marketing/products/*.png` (34 products) - Category backgrounds (dynamic SVG): `/images/categories/${slug}.svg` — slug values in `app/categoria/*/page.tsx` - Category heroes: heroImage prop → `app/categoria/*/page.tsx` → `/images/marketing/hero-*.png` - Hero carousel: content-driven array in `content/es.json` → `/images/marketing/hero-carousel-*.png` - Testimonials: `"image"` field in `content/es.json` → `/images/testimonials/avatar-*.png` - Logo: `header.tsx` → `/images/logo.png` (or `logo-v2.png` after cache-bust) - Favicon: `layout.tsx` → `/images/favicon.png` (or `favicon-v2.png`) - OG image: `layout.tsx` → `/images/og-viajero.svg` - PWA icons: `manifest.json` → `/images/icon-192.png`, `/images/icon-512.png` ### Generation Recipes **Category icons (128x128):** Create a Python script with `ImageDraw` — tent, fish, umbrella, backpack, car, motorcycle, barn silhouettes on green circle background. Tell the client these are placeholders for real customer photos. **PWA icons:** Render from the logo at 192x192 and 512x512 with anti-aliasing. ## Multi-Locale Content Updates (PAB Sites) When updating content across all locales (en/es/nl/de) in a PAB (Paragu-AI Builder) project: ### Safe approach vs. pitfalls 1. **Python JSON manipulation is safer than `patch` tool for large content changes.** The `patch` tool works well for small string replacements, but when replacing JSON blocks spanning 50+ lines, it can match the wrong text, leave orphaned content, and produce valid-but-wrong JSON. This happened when replacing the `processPage` section — patch matched a subset, leaving trailing orphan that was syntactically valid but structurally wrong. 2. **When `patch` goes wrong: stop, don't stack.** If the first patch produces unexpected results (orphan content, duplicated sections, lost keys), do NOT apply more patches on top. Switch immediately to Python `json.load()/json.dump()` — read the broken file, fix the data structure in memory, and write it back clean. Stacking patches compounds the mistakes and makes the file increasingly fragile to further edits. The `processPage` fix required three patches to clean up what one wrong match did. 3. **`patch` can match content from a previously-applied patch** — If a file was already modified, the old_string might not match what's actually in the file. Always read the file fresh before patching, and verify with a direct key inspection after (`python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('f')); print(d.get('targetKey',{}).keys())"`). 4. **Shell variable expansion fails in `execute_code`'s multi-line Python strings** — `for f in $FILES; do python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('$f'))"; done` breaks because `$f` isn't expanded inside the Python string. Use explicit file paths, a temp script, or Python's own `glob` module instead. 5. **Always validate JSON structure after content changes, not just syntax.** A file can be valid JSON but structurally wrong (missing keys, wrong step counts, orphan content). After validation: ```bash python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('file'))" # syntax check ``` Also verify structural integrity: ```python d["processPage"]["process"]["steps"] # should have 5 steps d["home"]["services"]["groups"] # should have 3 groups d["aboutPage"]["story"]["paragraphs"] # should have 3 paragraphs ``` 6. **When deleting orphaned content from a botched patch, read the file with JSON parser first**, then find the structural key that contains the orphan data. Delete it from the Python dict and re-write. Don't try to surgically remove lines with patch. 7. **Process step consistency rule:** The same process narrative lives in 6 places across the site. Update ALL of these together in the same script block — missing any one creates inconsistency: - `home.process.steps` (homepage) - `processPage.process.steps` (process page) - `landingInversor.process.steps`, `landingTrust.process.steps`, `landingLifestyle.process.steps`, `landingEmpresa.process.steps` (LinkedIn ad landing pages) 8. **Process for adding new content sections across locales:** - Step 1: Define the content data structure in a Python dict with all localizations - Step 2: Load each locale file, apply transformations, write back - Step 3: Validate JSON syntax for all files - Step 4: Verify structural keys match expectations (count groups, steps, paragraphs) ```python for lang in ["en", "es", "nl", "de"]: with open(f"content/{lang}.json") as f: d = json.load(f) d["parentSection"]["newKey"] = translations[lang] with open(f"content/{lang}.json", "w") as f: json.dump(d, f, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False) ``` 9. **Section structure for the 'Our Services' pattern:** ```python data["home"]["services"] = { "eyebrow": "Our Services", "title": "Section title", "groups": [ {"id": "group-id", "title": "Group Name", "subtitle": "Group subtitle.", "items": [{"title": "Service", "description": "Desc.", "icon": "IconName"}]} ] } ``` 10. **Section structure for the 'Story' pattern:** ```python data["aboutPage"]["story"] = { "title": "Our Story", "eyebrow": "Tagline", "paragraphs": ["Para 1.", "Para 2.", "Para 3."] } ``` 11. **Section structure for the 'Process steps' pattern:** ```python data["processPage"]["process"]["steps"] = [ {"number": 1, "title": "Step Name", "description": "Long description.", "icon": "IconName", "image": {"$img": "process.key"}, "duration": "Week 1"} ] ``` 12. **Landing page Calendly URL pattern:** ```python base = "https://calendly.com/nexaparaguay/consulta" utm = f"utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=paid&utm_campaign={campaign}" url = f"{base}?{utm}&utm_content=cta-{placement}-{lang}" ``` ## Deployment ### Docker Deployment After modifying content assets (images, content JSON, or static files): 1. **Rebuild** — `npm run build` catches missing images and type errors 2. **Rebuild Docker image** — `docker build -t elviajero-comercio:prod .` 3. **Deploy** — `docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml elviajero-comercio --detach=false` 4. **Verify** — `curl -sI https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com/ | head -5` 5. **Force rolling update** — if same image tag, `docker service update --force elviajero-comercio_web` Never assume changes to static content or images are live — Docker rebuild and stack deploy are required. Browser cache is NOT the server — always curl first. ### Production Monitoring After every deploy, add: - Healthcheck endpoint at `/api/health` returning `{status, timestamp, uptime}` - Docker HEALTHCHECK in Dockerfile (every 30s, 40s start grace, 3 retries) - Cron healthcheck via Hermes: every 15m, auto-redeploy on failure - Shell check script: `/root/scripts/check-{project}.sh` ### Docker Build Timeout `docker build` with npm install can take >120s on cold cache. Set terminal timeout to 300s for builds. If it times out, retry — the npm cache is warm on the second run and it will complete faster. #### Next.js `pages/` Folder Conflict (v16+) Next.js 16+ errors with "`pages` and `app` directories should be under the same folder" when a `pages/` data directory exists at project root alongside `src/app/`. Fix: 1. Rename `pages/` to `nexa-pages/` 2. Update all `page.tsx` references from `'pages'` to `'nexa-pages'` 3. Update Dockerfile to copy `nexa-pages/` into the standalone output 4. Run `docker build --no-cache` after rename (Docker layer cache may use old path) #### Docker Standalone + Data Files Next.js `output: "standalone"` produces a `server.js` that does `process.chdir(__dirname)` — so `process.cwd()` points to `.next/standalone/`, not the app root. Data files read at runtime via `readFileSync(path.join(process.cwd(), ...))` must be copied into `.next/standalone/`: ```dockerfile COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./ COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/nexa-pages ./.next/standalone/nexa-pages COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./.next/standalone/content ``` Without this, the container runs fine but returns static HTML with fallback data because `readFileSync` silently fails and the catch block returns defaults. #### The "Welcome" Bug (Static SSG Fallback) When a PAB page always renders "Welcome" regardless of content changes, the root cause is in the page component's fallback chain: ```tsx // BROKEN: home.json has 'titleKey' not 'title' — always falls through {homePage.title || content?.title || 'Welcome'} // FIXED: use siteName from the locale JSON {content?.siteName || 'Nexa Paraguay'} ``` Check that `getPages()` and `getContent()` actually read files via `readFileSync`/`readdirSync` at build time. The `catch` block silently swallowing the error is the symptom — verify the files are accessible during the Docker build stage. ## Communication Style (Ivan/ParaguAI) This user gives short commands and wants short responses. Key rules: - **No walls of text.** Deliver analysis in tables/bullets, not paragraphs. - **No explanations of what you're about to do.** Just do it and report what changed. - **Batch operations always.** Never one file at a time for repetitive work. - **One recommendation, not options.** Do not list alternatives unless asked. - **Final output: what changed, then stop.** No "Would you like me to...?" follow-ups. - **Deploy automatically.** If you changed something, build + deploy + verify without being asked. - **Hard refresh solves browser cache.** When user says "I still see old images", confirm server serves new file (curl), then tell them to hard refresh. Do NOT re-deploy. - **Phrasing: "Done." + summary + nothing else.** Test: if your response has more than 5 items of information after "Done.", it's too long. ## Full-Site Audit Methodology (PAB Static Sites) When performing a full site content audit on a PAB multi-locale site like nexa-paraguay, follow this 20-point hierarchy. Each finding gets: status emoji (✅/🟡/🔴), priority label, and actionable next step.
